question_text: Two objects, A and B, are dropped from the same height above Earth's surface in a vacuum. Object A has a mass of 2 kg and object B has a mass of 8 kg. Both experience the same gravitational field strength of 10 N/kg. Which of the following correctly describes their motion and the forces acting upon them?

options.label: A
options.text: Object B falls faster because it experiences a greater gravitational force, but both accelerate at the same rate due to gravity alone in a vacuum

options.label: B
options.text: Object A falls faster because it has less mass to overcome, and it accelerates more rapidly than object B

options.label: C
options.text: Object B experiences twice the gravitational force but accelerates at half the rate of object A

options.label: D
options.text: Both objects experience the same gravitational force and fall at different rates depending on their mass

correct_answer: Object B falls faster because it experiences a greater gravitational force, but both accelerate at the same rate due to gravity alone in a vacuum
explanation: Although object B (8 kg) experiences a greater gravitational force than object A (2 kg)—80 N compared to 20 N—both objects accelerate at the same rate in a vacuum. This is because acceleration depends on the ratio of force to mass (a = F/m). Since the gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg for both, they both accelerate at 10 m/s². Object B falls faster (has greater velocity) because it experiences more force, but they both accelerate at the same rate. In a vacuum, there is no air resistance, so mass does not affect acceleration due to gravity.
